metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify exactly where and under what config condition compute_plan_review_validity(close profile) is called in the close path (e.g., 'called in transition_close.close_ticket() before the locked write, guarded by verify.enforce_plan_material_pins, skipping check_code_freshness').
evidence: Codebase: transition_close.py:close_ticket() calls _completion_precheck() → llm.verify_completion(), not any plan-review compute_validity equivalent.
Codebase: plan_review_precheck in gates.py (the existing plan-review validity entry point) is only invoked on the claim/start-work path (open→in_progress), not on the close path.
Plan says 'Enabled pin drift invalidates the close-profile consumer when that gate is active' — but no existing gate makes this check on close, and the plan does not specify where or how to add one.

ABSENCE: no plan phrase specifies whether the close-profile check is guarded by an existing flag (require_completion_verification_for_close? require_plan_review_for_claim? a new flag?), or where in transition_close.py the compute_plan_review_validity call is inserted.
findings.finding: The plan's close-profile consumer is under-specified: the current close path in transition_close.py does not invoke any plan-review validity check (it calls verify_completion, the LLM completion-verifier), so 'the close story must call this profile rather than reimplement validity' requires NEW wiring whose insertion point, config condition, and interaction with the existing completion-verifier gate are not described. Without this, the 'close profile' cannot be reliably implemented as a reuse rather than a duplication.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'the close profile sets check_code_freshness=false while retaining signature, registry, phase, own-material, and configured pin checks; the close story must call this profile rather than reimplement validity'
scenarios: Implementer adds a duplicate pin-drift check inside transition_close.py without calling compute_plan_review_validity, recreating exactly the duplication the plan says to avoid.
Implementer adds the call under the wrong config guard (e.g., require_completion_verification_for_close), making pin-drift enforcement on close conditional on the wrong flag.
AC 'Enabled pin drift invalidates the close-profile consumer when that gate is active' is untestable without knowing which gate guards the close-profile call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the manifest-line format (prefix string, field layout, and value vocabulary) for the `review_phase` and `priority_floor` lines that `manifest_review_phase()` and `manifest_priority_floor()` will parse, and co-locate the `compatible`/`incompatible`/`malformed` derivation rule with the `phase_status` field in DerivedPlanReviewHealth.
evidence: AC 15 names all three parsers: manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), manifest_pins()
The Implementation section describes the pin format implicitly via TargetPinDetail but provides zero definition for what manifest line a 'review_phase' or 'priority_floor' parser would read
Codebase search confirms none of the three functions exist: search for 'manifest_review_phase manifest_priority_floor manifest_pins' returns no hits
phase_status enum values (compatible/incompatible/malformed) appear in DerivedPlanReviewHealth but no manifest-line format is given that would allow manifest_review_phase() to be written
findings.finding: manifest_review_phase() and manifest_priority_floor() are named in AC 15 as functions the completion-verifier branch must NOT invoke, but neither their manifest-line format (prefix string, field layout), vocabulary, nor return values are defined anywhere in this plan. An executor must invent this spec.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 15: 'The completion-verifier branch never invokes manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins()'
scenarios: An implementor writing manifest_review_phase() has no spec for the manifest line prefix, field layout, or what values map to compatible/incompatible/malformed.
An implementor guarding the completion-verifier branch cannot determine what manifest lines to exclude without the format definition.
The phase-incompatibility ACs (16–20) cannot be tested without knowing what data triggers phase_status=incompatible.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the phase_status derivation rule to the Implementation section: specify what manifest data (line prefix, value set) is read and what comparison produces compatible/incompatible/malformed, co-located with the DerivedPlanReviewHealth schema definition.
evidence: Implementation: 'phase_status exactly compatible, incompatible, or malformed' — enum defined but derivation rule absent
ACs 16–20 assert behavior for 'phase-incompatible attestations' without defining what makes an attestation phase-incompatible
No manifest line format for 'review_phase' is defined anywhere in the plan, so there is no data source for the phase_status decision
findings.finding: The plan defines phase_status (compatible/incompatible/malformed) in DerivedPlanReviewHealth but never specifies the decision rule: what observable fact(s) about an attestation's manifest data cause phase_status to be 'incompatible' vs 'compatible' vs 'malformed'. Without this rule, ACs 16–20 (phase-incompatible attestations fail claim, close, reuse, re-sign, audit) are unimplementable.
findings.location: Implementation section — DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract; Acceptance Criteria AC 16–20
scenarios: An implementor cannot distinguish a 'compatible' from an 'incompatible' attestation without a rule.
Tests for ACs 16–20 cannot be written without knowing what fixture data triggers incompatible phase_status.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Implementation sub-section (or expand the health-contract description) that defines what constitutes a phase-incompatible attestation, what data is compared, and how phase_status is set to 'incompatible' vs 'malformed'.
evidence: Implementation names 'phase_status exactly compatible, incompatible, or malformed' as a field but provides zero description of the detection logic
ACs 16–20 presuppose a working phase-compatibility check that is never described in the plan body
ABSENCE: no section describes what a 'phase-incompatible' attestation is or how incompatibility is determined
findings.finding: ACs 16–20 require phase-incompatibility enforcement across all consumers (claim, close, reuse, re-sign, audit, derived health), but the Implementation body only names the phase_status field in the health contract without describing what constitutes a phase mismatch, how phase_status is populated, or what logic drives phase-incompatibility detection. There is no described deliverable implementing this behavior.
findings.location: Implementation (DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract description) vs. AC lines: 'Phase-incompatible attestations fail claim validity', 'Phase-incompatible attestations fail close-profile validity', 'Phase-incompatible attestations cannot be reused', 'Phase-incompatible attestations cannot be re-signed', 'Audit plus derived health report phase incompatibility'
scenarios: Implementer builds the contract shape but has no spec for when to set phase_status=incompatible, leaving phase enforcement as undefined behavior
Test author cannot write phase-incompatibility tests without an agreed definition of phase mismatch
findings.suggested_fix: Under the DerivedPlanReviewHealth description, add: 'phase_status is set by comparing the attestation's recorded phase against the current manifest phase at validation time; incompatible when they differ; malformed when the manifest phase field is unreadable/missing.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add a description of the 'completion-verifier branch' and its relationship to compute_plan_review_validity in the Implementation section, or move this context into the plan body so the AC is traceable to described work.
evidence: ABSENCE: 'completion-verifier branch' is not mentioned anywhere in the Goal, Implementation, or Verification sections
ABSENCE: manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), manifest_pins() are not mentioned in any other part of the plan
The Implementation describes a unified compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary but does not differentiate a 'completion-verifier branch' from it
findings.finding: The AC 'The completion-verifier branch never invokes manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins(); completion attestations carrying those unexpected lines do not raise ManifestFormatError' references a 'completion-verifier branch' and three named functions that appear nowhere in the Implementation body. There is no described deliverable corresponding to this criterion.
findings.location: AC: 'The completion-verifier branch never invokes manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins()...'
scenarios: Implementer has no context for what the completion-verifier branch is, cannot implement the described guard, and the AC becomes untestable
Reviewer cannot verify this AC is satisfied without external knowledge not present in the plan

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a per-AC (or per-AC-group) concrete proving command to the Verification section — e.g., `pytest tests/test_pin_health.py -k 'drift and claim'` — so every criterion has at least one falsifiable in-session check that exercises the new path.
evidence: 'Run focused attestation invalidation, reuse, drift-refresh, re-sign, and config tests.' — no test file names, test IDs, or CLI flags given.
'Run make check and the non-integration test suite.' — a universal lint/format/test command does not by itself prove any specific criterion per E6 rubric note.
ABSENCE: no AC line includes a `pytest -k`, `grep`, `jq`, or equivalent command that would produce falsifiable evidence for that criterion.
findings.finding: The Verification section provides only two generic 'run tests' bullets ('Run focused attestation invalidation, reuse, drift-refresh, re-sign, and config tests' and 'Run make check and the non-integration test suite') with no specific test names, file paths, or command invocations. None of the 20 acceptance criteria has a concrete proving command that would produce evidence on success, violating E6(a) and E6(c).
findings.location: Verification section; all 20 Acceptance Criteria lines
scenarios: An agent executing the plan cannot tell whether 'focused tests' for 'Enabled pin drift prevents idempotent attestation reuse' exists and passes without a specific test name or command.
'make check' passing does not distinguish between 'the drift-invalidation path is exercised' and 'the path is dead code but lint passes'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the persisted-manifest schema state after a code rollback: specify either (a) that pre-feature verifier binaries tolerate `plan-material-pin:` lines (with a deployed-binary compatibility test, not just a fixture under new code), or (b) a compensating-forward procedure (e.g., re-sign affected tickets after rollback); note any DATA LOSS RISK if old resign/refresh paths silently drop pin lines.
evidence: Plan adds new `plan-material-pin: <role> <id> <fingerprint>` lines inside the HMAC-signed manifest payload — a durable persisted-format change.

Plan specifies: 'A pre-feature reader authenticates the complete stored manifest, then ignores additive pin lines for field interpretation' — but this is a NEW-code-reads-OLD-manifest test, not a rollback-old-code-reads-new-manifest assertion.

ABSENCE: no plan section states what happens when rolled-back (pre-feature) resign_plan_review or refresh_attestation encounters a pin-bearing manifest — old build_manifest will silently drop pin lines, re-signing a pinless manifest.
ABSENCE: no back-out path (e.g., compensating-forward re-sign, or an explicit 'pre-feature verifier tolerates pin lines' guarantee for deployed binaries) is described.

T11(e) rubric: 'the plan must assert on the schema STATE AFTER rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0 — a rollback can exit 0 while leaving the schema in an incorrect intermediate state. Where destructiveness is ambiguous, default to compensating-forward and attach a literal DATA LOSS RISK note.'
'Do not rewrite pins when configuration changes' covers the enforcement-flag scenario only; it does not address rollback of the feature code itself.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `plan-material-pin:` lines into cryptographically-signed persisted manifests but never asserts the schema/manifest state AFTER a code rollback. If pre-feature code is redeployed after pin-bearing manifests exist: (1) old verifier code may reject manifests with unknown pin lines, rendering all pin-bearing attestations unverifiable and blocking every claim gate; (2) old resign_plan_review / refresh_attestation will silently emit a pinless manifest when re-signing, dropping pin provenance without error — DATA LOSS RISK. The plan's pre-feature-verifier fixture only covers NEW code reading OLD manifests, not the reverse.
findings.location: Implementation section ('Compare signed pins against current narrow material…') and AC line 'Missing/deleted, archived, malformed, duplicate, and re-enable scenarios are deterministic'; also 'Do not rewrite pins when configuration changes.'
scenarios: Feature is deployed; several tickets accumulate pin-bearing manifests. A production incident forces rollback to pre-feature code. Old claim-gate verifier code encounters manifests with unknown `plan-material-pin:` lines; if the old parser is not tolerant, verification fails for every pin-bearing attestation, blocking all claim gates until re-review.

After rollback, an operator calls resign_plan_review or drift refresh on an existing pin-bearing attestation. Old build_manifest emits a manifest without pin lines; the new HMAC signature covers a pinless manifest, silently discarding pin provenance — DATA LOSS RISK.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the decision rule for `current_fingerprint: null` in TargetPinDetail: enumerate the cases (deleted/missing → null + stale-pin-missing; archived → string fingerprint; I/O error → null + fail behavior) and state whether an I/O-error null fails open or closed.
evidence: TargetPinDetail specifies 'current_fingerprint: string|null' with no explanation of when null is used
Implementation states 'deleted/missing pinned target invalidate' and 'Archived targets remain readable' as separate cases
No rule specifies how to determine whether a null current_fingerprint came from an archived (readable) vs deleted (invalidating) target
findings.finding: The `current_fingerprint: string|null` field in TargetPinDetail is defined as null-capable, but the plan gives no decision rule distinguishing the null cases: I/O error on fingerprint recompute vs. target deleted vs. target archived. The enforcement branch ('deleted/missing pinned target invalidate') requires distinguishing deleted from archived, but both would present as null or unreadable without a disambiguation rule.
findings.location: Implementation — TargetPinDetail definition: 'current_fingerprint: string|null'; also 'Archived targets remain readable'
scenarios: A pinned target that has been archived returns a fingerprint; a deleted target returns null — but without a rule the executor cannot implement the distinction.
An I/O error during fingerprint computation would also yield null, and without a fallback rule it is ambiguous whether this is fail-open or fail-closed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly call out that the 'close profile' consumer requires adding a new plan-review validity gate branch to `close_ticket()` in `transition_close.py`, distinct from and additive to the existing completion-verification gate.
evidence: src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py lines 444–469 show close gate calls `_completion_precheck`, not any plan-review validity function
Grep of src/ for 'close.*plan.review', 'plan_review.*close_gate', 'plan-review.*close' returns zero hits
compute_validity() in attest.py has no 'close' kind/profile branch (only 'completion-verifier' and 'plan-review' at lines 519, 543)
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the close story must call this profile rather than reimplement validity' implying a plan-review validity check already exists on the close path. Reading `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` (the close gate) confirms it calls `_completion_precheck` (completion-verifier gate) — there is NO plan-review validity invocation anywhere in the close path. The plan treats the close-profile as an extension of an existing wiring, but it is a net-new gate addition.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'the close story must call this profile rather than reimplement validity'
scenarios: The plan implies there is a 'close story' already calling plan-review validity. No such consumer exists, meaning this is entirely new work: a new gate branch in `close_ticket`, a new invocation of `compute_plan_review_validity` with close profile, and new tests.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `compute_plan_review_validity` is a NEW function (rename/refactor of `compute_validity` with a `profile` parameter), enumerate all six consumer call-sites that must be migrated, and explicitly list wiring the close path to plan-review validity as a net-new gate rather than a 'profile' of an existing one.
evidence: Grep of src/ for 'compute_plan_review_validity' returns zero hits
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py line 471 defines `compute_validity()` (no profile param)

plan_review/__init__.py line 454 calls `claim_gate_check`, resign.py line 126 calls `sign_plan_review` directly, drift_refresh_candidate at line 146 in attest.py does not call any unified profile boundary
Plan states 'the close story must call this profile rather than reimplement validity' but transition_close.py has no plan-review validity call at all
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary used by claim, reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit/health, and close' as if `compute_plan_review_validity` already exists. Grep confirms the real function is `compute_validity()` in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py`. There is no `compute_plan_review_validity`, no 'profile' parameter on `compute_validity`, and no single boundary shared across all six consumers — each consumer calls `compute_validity` directly or via `claim_gate_check`. The plan's entire profile-boundary strategy rests on renaming/extending this existing function, but it describes the target as if already present.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary used by claim, reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit/health, and close.'
scenarios: A developer implementing this plan will find no existing `compute_plan_review_validity` to extend and must create it from scratch, wire all six consumers, and introduce the profile parameter — significantly more work than the plan implies.
If the close consumer is not currently wired to plan-review validity (confirmed: transition_close.py has no such call), the plan silently drops that wiring work from its implementation steps.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary…' with 'Rename/extend `compute_validity` → `compute_plan_review_validity(attestation, ticket_state, profile: Literal["claim","close"], *, repo_root) → {valid, reason, verdict, health}`. Migrate each of the six consumers (claim via `claim_gate_check`, `_idempotent_reuse`, `drift_refresh_candidate`, `resign_plan_review`, audit/health, and the new close gate) to call this single function.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the retirement plan for `compute_validity`: either (a) rename/extend it in-place so all existing call-sites gain pin-enforcement automatically, or (b) explicitly list the migration of all 10+ call-sites to `compute_plan_review_validity` as part of this story's scope.
evidence: compute_validity exists at src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:471 with 10+ active call-sites: claim_gate_check (attest.py:759), _attested_delivered (attest.py:653), completion.child_closure_findings (completion.py:176), drift_floor (drift_floor.py multiple), signing.py references, _opcert_signing.py references.
Plan says 'Own one … boundary' but does not state that compute_validity is removed/aliased.
ABSENCE: no plan statement that compute_validity is deprecated, aliased to the new function, or that its call-sites are migrated.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `compute_plan_review_validity` as a new named boundary but never states whether the existing `compute_validity` (attest.py:471, 10+ call-sites) is retired or survives in parallel. If both coexist, the new function starts at zero call-sites (premature abstraction) and duplicates the existing validity logic (NIH rebuild of a production function).
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary used by claim, reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit/health, and close.'
scenarios: If compute_validity survives alongside compute_plan_review_validity, the 10 existing call-sites bypass pin-enforcement forever — the plan's pin-invalidation ACs can never be fully satisfied.
If both coexist with identical logic, it is an NIH duplication of a production boundary.
If compute_plan_review_validity is built first without migrating call-sites, it is a premature abstraction with zero real call-sites at the time it ships.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `compute_validity` in-place under its existing name (adding a `profile=` keyword parameter and the pin/phase checks), keeping it as the single boundary. Alias `compute_plan_review_validity = compute_validity` for any new callers that want the explicit name. This avoids a parallel-function split and guarantees every existing call-site gets pin-enforcement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either define `ManifestFormatError` as a new exception class and specify the exact parse conditions that raise it, or remove it from AC14/AC15 and restate those ACs in terms of the `malformed-pin` / `malformed` status values the `DerivedPlanReviewHealth` contract already enumerates.
evidence: Grep of src/ for 'ManifestFormatError' returns zero hits
Grep of src/ for 'manifest_review_phase', 'manifest_priority_floor', 'manifest_pins' each returns zero hits
manifest.py (the manifest parser module) has no FormatError, only returns None/defaults on missing lines
findings.finding: The plan asserts `ManifestFormatError` exists (AC14, AC15: 'do not raise ManifestFormatError', 'malformed manifest metadata returns malformed/invalid…without raising'). Grep of the entire src/ tree returns zero hits for `ManifestFormatError`. This is a new exception class the plan introduces, but ACs 14–15 reference it as if it already exists and has a defined raise contract.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC14 'Malformed manifest metadata returns malformed/invalid at every consumer without raising.' and AC15 'The completion-verifier branch never invokes manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins(); completion attestations carrying those unexpected lines do not raise ManifestFormatError.'
scenarios: A test author implementing AC15 has no pre-existing raise contract to test against — they must first define what conditions cause ManifestFormatError and where it is raised, which the plan does not specify.

AC14 says 'without raising' but no existing code raises anything on malformed lines (they silently return None/defaults), so the AC is vacuously true today and only becomes meaningful once the new parsers are introduced.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state whether `manifest_review_phase()`, `manifest_priority_floor()`, and `manifest_pins()` are created by this ticket or a predecessor, and name the module they live in (e.g., `manifest.py`) as part of the edit-target list.
evidence: No matches for `manifest_review_phase`, `manifest_priority_floor` anywhere in the repo source
`manifest_pins` appears only in cache/plan-review review notes, not in production source
The AC presupposes these symbols exist (or will exist after this ticket), but neither their origin module nor their creation scope is stated
findings.finding: The new symbols `manifest_review_phase()`, `manifest_priority_floor()`, and `manifest_pins()` are referenced in AC 'The completion-verifier branch never invokes manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins()' but do not exist in the codebase (`manifest.py`, `attest.py`). The plan neither names which module they live in nor lists them as edit targets for creation, making their provenance ambiguous — are they created by this ticket or a dependency?
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'The completion-verifier branch never invokes manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins()'
scenarios: If these symbols come from a dependency ticket and are not yet created, the AC is untestable. If they are created by this ticket, the plan is missing them from the edit targets / Implementation description.
findings.suggested_fix: If they are new functions in `manifest.py` created by this ticket, add them to the Implementation's edit-target description alongside the module name.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pre-flight / dry-run mode (or an audit-only path that reports how many existing attestations would be invalidated) so operators can assess blast radius before enabling enforcement, and document this as the recommended enablement procedure.
evidence: Plan protects only 'legacy unpinned' attestations on toggle: 'Treat a wholly unpinned legacy attestation as valid in either mode'
No pre-flight audit step, dry-run flag, or operator warning specified before enabling enforcement
Pinned-but-drifted attestations become immediately invalid on enforcement enable with no transition window
'once enforcement is successfully enabled, validation fails closed' confirms the hard flip
findings.finding: The plan protects 'legacy unpinned' attestations when enforcement is toggled on, but does not address the blast-radius risk for already-pinned-but-drifted attestations in an existing deployment: enabling `enforce_plan_material_pins=true` could silently mass-invalidate a large cohort of previously-valid attestations with no migration window, operator warning, or pre-flight dry-run step specified.
findings.location: Implementation — 'When enabled, drift, malformed pins, or a deleted/missing pinned target invalidate the attestation' and AC 'Legacy unpinned attestations remain valid across configuration toggles'
scenarios: Operator enables enforcement on a production deployment; hundreds of existing pinned attestations with minor drift become invalid simultaneously; all dependent operations (claim, close, reuse) fail; no rollback guidance exists beyond 'toggle the flag back'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=audit` (or `dry_run`) mode that runs all validation checks and reports outcomes without changing `valid`; document the recommended enablement sequence as: (1) run audit mode to enumerate drift, (2) remediate or accept, (3) enable enforcement. Alternatively, add an AC item: 'Enabling enforcement when drifted pinned attestations exist emits a structured count/warning before first failure.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/audit/read.py` (the `audit_trail` aggregator) as an explicit edit target, specifying what new field(s) it must return to satisfy the 'audit reports pin drift / phase incompatibility as invalid' ACs.
evidence: src/rebar/audit/read.py: `audit_trail` returns `{ticket, plan_reviews, completion, code_reviews}` — no health/validity field
`plan_reviews` = `plan_sidecar.all_review_results(...)` (raw sidecar, no computed health)
No call to `compute_validity` / `compute_plan_review_validity` anywhere in `audit/read.py`
findings.finding: `audit/read.py` is not named as an edit target, but the ACs require 'Audit reports enabled pin drift as invalid' and 'Audit plus derived health report phase incompatibility'. The existing `audit_trail` function returns `plan_reviews` (raw sidecar history) and has no `health` or validity field; it does not call `compute_plan_review_validity`.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Audit reports enabled pin drift as invalid' and 'Audit plus derived health report phase incompatibility'
scenarios: The audit surface will not reflect pin-drift invalidity or phase incompatibility at all unless `audit/read.py` is updated — the ACs will be untestable against the existing audit consumer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` and/or `src/rebar/_commands/gates.py` as edit targets for the close-profile plan-review validity wiring, and describe where in the close flow the new profile check is inserted.
evidence: src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py: `close_ticket` calls `_completion_precheck` for completion-verifier but has NO call to any plan-review validity check
src/rebar/_commands/gates.py:115: only `claim_gate_check` is wired; no close-profile plan-review gate exists
Plan's close-profile is new behavior (no existing close-path plan-review validity call exists)
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` and `src/rebar/_commands/gates.py` are not named as edit targets, but the plan mandates a 'close profile' of `compute_plan_review_validity` that the 'close story must call rather than reimplement validity.' No close-path plan-review validity check exists today in either file; adding it requires modifying these files.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'The close profile sets check_code_freshness=false ... the close story must call this profile rather than reimplement validity'; AC 'Enabled pin drift invalidates the close-profile consumer when that gate is active'
scenarios: Without naming these files as edit targets, the close-profile AC ('Enabled pin drift invalidates the close-profile consumer') has no implementation path, and the plan's unified validity contract cannot be wired to the close consumer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback or expand-contract strategy for the compute_plan_review_validity consolidation (e.g., keep the per-consumer logic in place behind a flag until the unified boundary is validated, or describe an explicit revert procedure if the unified boundary causes regressions).
evidence: 'the close story must call this profile rather than reimplement validity' implies close currently does not call this function — a behavioral change
No section describes what happens if the unified boundary introduces a regression in any of the six consumers
ABSENCE: no rollback, feature-gate, or expand-contract sequencing for the validity-boundary consolidation
T4 requires an explicit rollback/back-out plan for changes consumers rely on, not just acknowledgement
findings.finding: The plan unifies validity checking into a single compute_plan_review_validity boundary and requires the close story to 'call this profile rather than reimplement validity' — a behavioral refactor touching claim, reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit/health, and close. No rollback path or expand-contract sequencing is described for this refactoring, despite it being a breaking internal change for any consumer that currently has its own validity logic.
findings.location: Implementation: 'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary used by claim, reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit/health, and close. The close story must call this profile rather than reimplement validity.'
scenarios: A regression in compute_plan_review_validity affects all six consumers simultaneously with no isolation mechanism
The close consumer, forced off its existing validity path, may silently change behavior if the unified profile differs in edge cases

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the plan title and goal statement to explicitly name phase-incompatibility enforcement and completion-verifier branch isolation as in-scope deliverables, or extract them into a separate plan.
evidence: Title: 'Derive plan-material-pin health and enforce optional drift invalidation'

ACs include: 'Phase-incompatible attestations fail claim validity', 'Phase-incompatible attestations fail close-profile validity', 'Phase-incompatible attestations cannot be reused', 'Phase-incompatible attestations cannot be re-signed', 'Audit plus derived health report phase incompatibility'
AC: 'The completion-verifier branch never invokes manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins()'
These items are not mentioned in the title or goal statement and represent independent behavior additions beyond pin-health/drift enforcement
findings.finding: The plan's title promises 'derive plan-material-pin health and enforce optional drift invalidation,' but the body and ACs add substantial scope beyond that: phase-incompatibility as a validity failure (five ACs) and completion-verifier branch isolation (one AC barring manifest_review_phase/manifest_priority_floor/manifest_pins calls). These are meaningful behavior changes not signaled by the title, meaning an implementer reading only the title would not expect them.
findings.location: Title vs. Acceptance Criteria (Phase-incompatible ACs: 'Phase-incompatible attestations fail claim validity' through 'Audit plus derived health report phase incompatibility'; Completion-verifier AC: 'The completion-verifier branch never invokes manifest_review_phase()...')
scenarios: An implementer scoping work from the title alone would not implement phase-incompatibility enforcement or completion-verifier branch isolation, leaving five passing ACs unaddressed and a discrepancy between delivered scope and title expectations.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the title to something like 'Derive plan-material-pin health, enforce optional drift invalidation, and add phase-incompatibility validity enforcement' and add a sentence to the Goal section noting the completion-verifier isolation requirement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `rebar/llm/completion.py` as an explicit edit target (it calls `compute_validity` with `kind="completion-verifier"`) and confirm the rename/replacement of `compute_validity` → `compute_plan_review_validity` preserves the completion-verifier dispatch path.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/completion.py:176: `v = compute_validity(sig, c, "completion-verifier", repo_root=repo_root)`
`compute_plan_review_validity` does not exist in the codebase; `compute_validity` at attest.py:471 is the current symbol being evolved
Plan lists consumers as: claim, reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit/health, and close — `completion.py` is absent from this list
findings.finding: `compute_validity` (the existing function in `attest.py:471`) is called by `rebar/llm/completion.py:176` (outside the `plan_review/` dir) with `kind="completion-verifier"`. The plan renames/replaces this function with `compute_plan_review_validity` but does not name `rebar/llm/completion.py` as an edit target, leaving it with a broken call-site.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary'
scenarios: After the rename, `rebar.llm.completion`'s child-closure findings will crash at runtime with NameError/ImportError, breaking the completion-verifier close gate.
findings.suggested_fix: Either keep `compute_validity` as a thin shim that delegates to `compute_plan_review_validity`, or add `completion.py` to the edit targets list with an explicit note that the completion-verifier `kind` dispatch is unchanged.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the 'Missing/deleted, archived, malformed, duplicate, and re-enable scenarios are deterministic' AC into per-sub-scenario ACs each naming the expected pin_status, valid flag, and verdict (e.g., 'duplicate pin entries in a target list produce pin_status=malformed-pin and, when enforced, valid=false').
evidence: 'Missing/deleted, archived, malformed, duplicate, and re-enable scenarios are deterministic.' — 'deterministic' names a property, not an observable outcome; none of the five sub-scenarios has a stated expected value for pin_status, valid, or verdict.
ABSENCE: no prose anywhere in the plan defines what 'duplicate' means in the pin context or what 're-enable' means operationally.
findings.finding: The AC 'Missing/deleted, archived, malformed, duplicate, and re-enable scenarios are deterministic' bundles at least five distinct sub-scenarios under the word 'deterministic' without stating the expected observable outcome for any of them. An executor cannot write correct test assertions without knowing: what a 'duplicate' pin means (two targets with the same canonical_id?), what 're-enable' means (re-enabling enforcement after it was disabled?), and what the expected pin_status, valid flag, and verdict are in each case.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Missing/deleted, archived, malformed, duplicate, and re-enable scenarios are deterministic.'
scenarios: An executor writing a 'duplicate pin' test doesn't know whether to expect pin_status=malformed-pin, an error, or a deduplicated-but-valid result.

An executor writing a 're-enable' test doesn't know whether an attestation pinned while enforcement was off should be treated as legacy-unpinned or stale-pin-drift when enforcement is later turned on.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the single omnibus AC with five explicit ACs:
- Deleted/missing pinned target → pin_status=stale-pin-missing, enforced→invalid, disabled→advisory.
- Archived pinned target → readable, pin_status=current (or stale if fingerprint changed), not invalidated.
- Malformed pin (unparseable) → pin_status=malformed-pin, enforced→invalid.
- Duplicate canonical_id entries → pin_status=malformed-pin, enforced→invalid.
- Re-enable (enforcement turned on after attestation was created without pins) → treated as legacy-unpinned, remains valid.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence Implementation note explicitly stating that verify.enforce_plan_material_pins governs only validity enforcement and has no effect on pin emission, so these ACs are traceable to a described design decision.
evidence: Implementation says 'Add verify.enforce_plan_material_pins with default false and strict enabled-mode validation' — scope is validity checking only
ABSENCE: pin emission is not described anywhere in Goal, Implementation, or Verification
The ACs verify a constraint on out-of-scope behavior with no described work to trace to
findings.finding: Two ACs — 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true leaves new pin emission enabled' and 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=false leaves new pin emission enabled' — verify that pin emission behavior is unaffected by the new flag, but the Implementation body describes only the flag's effect on validity checking; pin emission logic is never described as in-scope work. These ACs are orphaned from any described deliverable.
findings.location: AC lines: 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true leaves new pin emission enabled', 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=false leaves new pin emission enabled'
scenarios: Implementer may inadvertently couple the enforcement flag to pin emission if there is no explicit in-plan statement separating them
These ACs cannot be linked to a specific implementation step for review or sign-off
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Implementation: 'The enforce_plan_material_pins flag controls only validity enforcement; it does not alter pin emission logic, which remains active regardless of this setting.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or AGENTS.md section capturing the rationale for the unified compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary and the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract shape (including why close uses check_code_freshness=false and why legacy-unpinned has an empty targets list).
evidence: No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc in Implementation or Verification sections
Novel DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract with exact field enumerations is a new public interface with no recorded rationale
Six consumers unified under one profile is a non-trivial architectural decision
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel cross-cutting architectural decision (a single compute_plan_review_validity profile contract replacing per-consumer validity logic, plus the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract) but specifies no update to AGENTS.md, an ADR, or any design document. Future maintainers refactoring any of the 6 consumers will have no recorded rationale for the unified boundary or the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract shape.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary used by claim, reuse, drift refresh, re-sign, audit/health, and close.'
scenarios: A developer later adds a seventh consumer and doesn't know why close uses check_code_freshness=false while claim does not
A reviewer trying to understand pin-status enum values has no design rationale to consult
findings.suggested_fix: Add a doc/adr/NNNN-plan-material-pin-health.md (or equivalent AGENTS.md section) covering: (1) why a single profile boundary was chosen over per-consumer logic, (2) the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract fields and their invariants, (3) the fail-open/fail-closed duality, and reference it from the Implementation section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify at least one instrumentation point (structured log entry or counter metric) emitted when enforcement-triggered invalidation occurs (pin drift, malformed pin, phase incompatibility, missing target) so operators can monitor and alert on enforcement-driven failure rates.
evidence: No metric, structured log, or alert is specified for enforcement-triggered invalidation events
AC: 'No warning event is persisted for derived health' — confirms intentional suppression of event persistence, leaving a gap for operator visibility
DerivedPlanReviewHealth is queryable but not push-based; operators cannot detect a sudden wave of enforcement failures without polling
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new enforcement-triggered invalidation path (pin drift, malformed pins, phase incompatibility blocking claim/reuse/re-sign/close) but specifies no observability instrumentation — no metric, structured log event, or alert — for operators to monitor enforcement-driven failures in production. The only observability mentioned is advisory warnings for unreadable config and the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract (queryable on demand), which is insufficient for detecting systematic enforcement failures at scale.
findings.location: Implementation — 'When enabled, drift, malformed pins, or a deleted/missing pinned target invalidate the attestation' and Acceptance Criteria — 'Enabled pin drift invalidates the claim consumer'
scenarios: verify.enforce_plan_material_pins is enabled org-wide; a bulk pin-drift event silently blocks all claim operations; no alert fires
A malformed-pin condition affects 30% of attestations; operators have no metric to detect the regression
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section, add: 'Emit a structured log entry at WARN level (or increment a labelled counter metric) each time enforcement causes an invalidation, including fields: consumer (claim|reuse|drift_refresh|re_sign|close|audit), pin_status, phase_status, and attestation canonical_id. This enables alert rules and dashboards without persisting warning events to the attestation record.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback procedure to the plan: state that setting `enforce_plan_material_pins=false` is sufficient, enumerate any in-flight operation states that require manual recovery, and add a test case covering the disable→enable→disable cycle with drifted attestations.
evidence: No rollback or undo section exists in the plan
AC 're-enable scenarios are deterministic' hints at re-enable but does not define rollback testing
'once enforcement is successfully enabled, validation fails closed' creates a sharp failure mode with no stated recovery path
findings.finding: Rollback to the non-enforcing state is only implicit (toggle the flag back); the plan names no explicit, tested rollback procedure, no indication of what state (if any) must be cleaned up, and no guidance on whether in-flight operations that failed under enforcement need manual recovery.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Keep optional unreadable configuration fail-open with a warning' / no explicit rollback section
scenarios: Enforcement is enabled; downstream operations start failing; on-call engineer needs to roll back quickly; no documented procedure exists for verifying the rollback is complete or whether any side-effects (e.g., failed close operations, orphaned claims) need manual remediation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection: 'Set verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=false to immediately restore advisory-only mode; no data cleanup is required; in-flight operations that returned an invalidation error must be retried by the caller.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` is read once per logical validation operation (not re-read mid-execution), and document who holds write authority over this config key.
evidence: No ownership or update-governance statement for the config key
'Keep optional unreadable configuration fail-open with a warning' addresses read failure but not concurrent update during a validation run
No statement on whether config is read once per request or cached, creating potential TOCTOU: config could flip mid-validation
findings.finding: The plan does not specify who owns updates to the `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` configuration key, the governance process for changing it, or how concurrent reads during a live config update are handled (e.g., whether a validator mid-execution can observe a partial or inconsistent config state).
findings.location: Implementation — 'Add verify.enforce_plan_material_pins with default false'
scenarios: Config is updated from false to true while a batch of attestation validations is in-flight; some validations see the old value, some see the new value, producing inconsistent results within the same operation batch.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Implementation: 'The enforcement flag is read once at the start of each compute_plan_review_validity invocation and held constant for that call; callers cannot change it mid-execution. Config ownership belongs to [deployer/operator]; no application code mutates this key at runtime.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'Motivation / Current Behavior' section that demonstrates the need: show what currently happens when pin drift occurs (Expected-vs-Actual or a failing scenario), name the defect or gap the current validity check leaves open, or cite the user/system problem that enforce_plan_material_pins solves.
evidence: Goal: 'Use signed pins as the single related-material validity check across every attestation lifecycle consumer' — states desired end-state, not a problem with the current state
No section explains what currently happens when pin drift occurs (e.g. 'today drift goes undetected'), why the existing validity check is insufficient, or what failure mode the new mechanism prevents
No reproduction steps, no Expected/Actual, no named defect or bug report referenced
ABSENCE: no 'currently X causes Y problem' or 'we observed Z failure' anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan adds a significant mechanism (DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract, enforce_plan_material_pins flag, unified validity profile boundary, phase checks) without demonstrating that current behavior is wrong or insufficient — no reproduction steps, no Expected-vs-Actual, no named defect, and no stated user/system problem the current behavior causes.
findings.location: Goal / Implementation sections
scenarios: If the current system already handles pin drift adequately under some path, the new enforcement layer may be redundant or introduce regressions without a demonstrated gap to close.
Without a motivating failure mode, reviewers and implementers cannot verify the mechanism addresses a real need or calibrate its scope.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief 'Current behavior / Problem' section, e.g.: 'Currently, pin drift across related materials is not enforced at any attestation lifecycle gate — a consumer can claim, reuse, or re-sign an attestation even when pinned targets have drifted. This allows stale or mismatched material to be treated as valid. The enforce_plan_material_pins flag and DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract close this gap.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider whether the completion-verifier ManifestFormatError isolation (AC 15) should be extracted as a prerequisite fix delivered ahead of the enforcement feature, or explicitly justify why it must ship together (e.g. it is prerequisite infrastructure for the unified profile boundary).
evidence: This AC prevents a distinct exception class (ManifestFormatError) that is unrelated to pin drift or phase-compatibility enforcement
It is independently testable (a unit test for the completion-verifier branch with a completion attestation carrying those lines) and independently releasable

Mixing a bug-fix (unexpected ManifestFormatError on completion attestations) with a new enforcement feature (pin drift invalidation) gives the ticket two reasons to change: 'completion-verifier is broken' and 'enforcement is missing'
findings.finding: The plan mixes a bug-fix concern (completion-verifier branch must not invoke manifest-only methods to avoid ManifestFormatError) with the new pin-health-enforcement feature, even though the former is independently valuable, testable, and releasable on its own.
findings.location: AC line: 'The completion-verifier branch never invokes manifest_review_phase(), manifest_priority_floor(), or manifest_pins(); completion attestations carrying those unexpected lines do not raise ManifestFormatError.'
scenarios: If the ManifestFormatError fix is blocked by the larger enforcement refactor, a live bug remains unaddressed longer than necessary.
If the enforcement refactor introduces a regression in the completion-verifier path, it is harder to bisect without the fix isolated.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that adding `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` explicitly calls out the three required changes: adding the field to `VerifyConfig`, adding a `_as_bool` coercer entry to `_SECTIONS['verify']`, and adding an env-override alias to `_CANONICAL_ENV_NAMES`.
evidence: Grep of src/ for 'enforce_plan_material_pins' returns zero hits
src/rebar/_config_schema.py VerifyConfig dataclass (lines 210-328) has no such field
src/rebar/_config_schema.py _SECTIONS['verify'] dict (lines 571-588) has no such key
findings.finding: The plan asserts `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` exists as a config key with 'default false'. Grep and reading `_config_schema.py` confirms this key does not exist in `VerifyConfig` or `_SECTIONS['verify']`. The plan treats it as a pre-existing key being configured, but it is entirely new schema work.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Add verify.enforce_plan_material_pins with default false and strict enabled-mode validation.'
scenarios: Implementing this requires adding a field to VerifyConfig, a coercer entry in _SECTIONS['verify'], and documentation — none of which is called out as a deliverable step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify and cite the specific existing gate code that implements the 'fail-open with warning on unreadable config' posture the plan claims to be matching, or acknowledge this is a new pattern and justify it independently.
evidence: Grep of src/ for 'fail.open', 'fail_open', 'fail open' returns zero hits
src/rebar/_config_schema.py _as_bool raises ConfigError on invalid input (fail-closed behavior)
src/rebar/_commands/gates.py loads config without a fail-open posture visible in surrounding code
No existing gate that loads config with a warn-and-continue on read failure was found
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Keep optional unreadable configuration fail-open with a warning, matching existing gate posture.' This implies an existing fail-open configuration read posture for gates. Grep of src/ for 'fail-open', 'fail_open', or 'optional.*config.*warn' returns zero matches. The `VerifyConfig` uses `_as_bool` coercers that RAISE `ConfigError` on invalid values (fail-closed). There is no documented 'existing gate posture' of failing open with a warning on config read errors.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Keep optional unreadable configuration fail-open with a warning, matching existing gate posture; once enforcement is successfully enabled, validation fails closed.'
scenarios: If the 'existing gate posture' the plan references does not actually exist, the implementer must design and build the fail-open behavior from scratch rather than reusing a pattern.
The plan's rationale for fail-open ('matching existing') may be incorrect, which could affect the security review of this design decision.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale for why DerivedPlanReviewHealth is a separate nested struct returned beside {valid, reason, verdict} rather than extending the existing validity contract inline (e.g., 'kept separate to preserve the stable {valid, reason, verdict} contract consumed by existing callers while making the new health detail opt-in for new consumers').
evidence: The plan defines a rich DerivedPlanReviewHealth with pin_status, phase_status, enforced, and targets but gives no rationale for why this is a parallel return value vs. extending the existing verdict/reason/verdict dict.

The existing compute_validity in attest.py returns a plain {valid, reason, verdict} dict; the plan adds a fourth key 'health' as a sibling — a structural choice with no stated justification (simplicity? extensibility? schema stability?).
ABSENCE of rationale: no plan phrase explains the 'beside' boundary decision.
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for representing pin-health as a distinct nested DerivedPlanReviewHealth struct returned beside the existing {valid, reason, verdict} dict, rather than extending the existing verdict contract inline. The absence of this rationale leaves implementers without grounding for the interface-boundary choice.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own the nested DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract returned as health beside compute_plan_review_validity existing top-level {valid, reason, verdict}'
scenarios: An implementer unsure whether 'beside' means a new top-level key on the same dict, a second return value (tuple), or a wrapper object may choose differently from what the plan intends.
Future contributors may merge the two shapes when they see no rationale for keeping them separate, causing quiet drift.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section, after the DerivedPlanReviewHealth definition, add one sentence: 'DerivedPlanReviewHealth is a separate nested return value (not merged into {valid, reason, verdict}) so existing callers of compute_plan_review_validity remain unchanged and the richer pin/phase detail is opt-in for consumers that need it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge and explicitly justify the custom `plan-material-pin:` manifest-line format against the in-toto materials-field / SLSA provenance prior art already cited in ADR 0044, 0046, and 0049 — or confirm alignment with the project's existing 'shaped as in-toto-style subject so a future DSSE move is an envelope swap' pattern (signing.py lines 229–231).
evidence: 'plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>' — a custom plaintext manifest-line format
ADR 0049 Prior art: 'in-toto / SLSA — subject/state binding; non-falsifiable provenance produced by a trusted control plane'
ADR 0044: 'DSSE is the Sigstore / in-toto / SLSA standard'
signing.py lines 229–231: 'The step is shaped as an in-toto-style subject so a future move to a DSSE/asymmetric envelope is an envelope swap, not a data-shape rewrite'

ABSENCE: no mention in the plan of why pin lines diverge from the in-toto materials-field pattern that ADR 0046 and ADR 0049 already recognise as directly applicable prior art for exactly this subject/state-binding use case
findings.finding: The plan introduces a custom plaintext 'plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>' manifest-line format to record which related-ticket material a review relied on — the same problem in-toto link attestation 'materials' fields solve. The project's own ADRs 0044, 0046, and 0049 explicitly cite in-toto/SLSA 'subject/state binding' as prior art; signing.py already adopts the 'shaped as in-toto-style subject so a future DSSE/asymmetric move is an envelope swap' pattern for verified_at_sha (signing.py lines 229–231). The plan neither acknowledges this established prior art for the pin-line encoding nor justifies why the new format diverges from the in-toto materials-field shape already used elsewhere in the codebase.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Own the nested DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract … TargetPinDetail is exactly {canonical_id, role, pinned_fingerprint, current_fingerprint, pin_status}'
scenarios: A future migration to asymmetric/DSSE attestations for plan-review manifests must handle pin lines that are opaque text rather than structured in-toto materials, requiring an additional bespoke parser on top of the envelope swap.

An external auditor familiar with in-toto/SLSA provenance records cannot use standard tooling to verify pin bindings because they are encoded as freeform text rather than in-toto Statement materials fields.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief design note to the Implementation section explaining why the pin lines are plaintext manifest steps (same transport as existing dep/regver lines, no new key custody, additive/backward-compatible) rather than in-toto materials fields, citing the same rationale signing.py already documents for verified_at_sha_step. Alternatively, align the pin encoding with the in-toto subject shape used by verified_at_sha_subject() so a future DSSE migration is still an envelope swap.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
